data.title: S&P 500 jumps 2% and these 20 stocks are fueling the rally
data.description:
U.S. equities advanced sharply on Tuesday, with the S&P 500 (SP500) climbing 2% to trade near 6,954, bringing the benchmark within 0.6% of its all-time high of 7,002.28 set on January 28.

The rally marks a significant recovery in market sentiment, as the index has now fully retraced losses incurred during the recent U.S.-Iran conflict.

Strength across major sectors has supported the move, reflecting renewed investor confidence and easing geopolitical concerns. With momentum building, market participants are closely watching whether the S&P 500 can decisively break above its previous record in the near term. 

Outlined below are the top 20 performing stocks within the S&P 500 on Tuesday:

  1. Robinhood Markets (HOOD), +9.9% 
  2. Delta Air Lines (DAL), +7.6%
  3. Coinbase Global (COIN), +6.9% 
  4. Super Micro Computer (SMCI), +6.6%
  5. Southwest Airlines (LUV), +6.3% 
  6. Axon Enterprise (AXON), +5.7%
  7. Carvana (CVNA), +5.6%
  8. Ares Management (ARES), +5.6%
  9. TransDigm Group (TDG), +5.2% 
  10. Bio-Techne (TECH), +4.7%
  11. BlackRock (BLK), +4.7%
  12. Meta Platforms (META), +4.6% 
  13. Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH), +4.6%
  14. United Airlines (UAL), +4.5% 
  15. Expedia Group (EXPE), +4.4%
  16. Oracle (ORCL), +4.4% 
  17. Blackstone (BX), +4.2%
  18. Carnival (CCL), +4.% 
  19. Apollo Global Management (APO), +3.9%
  20. Ford (F), +3.9% 

S&P 500 Funds: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).

data.title: Southern Copper CEO Rocha dies unexpectedly
data.description:
Southern Copper (SCCO) said late Tuesday that President, CEO, and board member Oscar Gonzalez Rocha died unexpectedly on April 7 and plans to appoint a successor or interim CEO in the near future.

Rocha was Southern Copper's (SCCO) President and CEO since 2004 and was a director of the company since 1999, after serving as President and CEO of Americas Mining Corp. and CEO and a director of Asarco LLC.

Rocha also headed Grupo Mexico's (GMBXF) mining division, consolidating the conglomerate's position as one of the most important mining groups in the region.

Southern Copper (SCCO), one of the world's largest copper producers, is 88.9%-owned by Grupo Mexico (GMBXF).

data.title: Equity longs ease as Hormuz blockade reshapes positioning, oil bets build
data.description:
Long positioning across U.S. equity indices has softened as traders reassess risk amid the ongoing US blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, while bullish bets on oil continue to build.

According to data from Capital.com, the share of traders long the S&P 500 (SP500) has slipped to 61% from 64% earlier in the week, while positioning in the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) dropped to 60% from 67%. The Russell 2000 stands at 52%, close to flipping net short, and the Nasdaq 100 (COMP:IND) has moved into a slight bearish bias.

Positioning is more mixed globally. Long exposure in the DAX has eased, while traders increased bullish bets on the ASX 200 and Nikkei 225.

In commodities, Capital.com data shows long positions in gold have been trimmed on profit-taking, while WTI crude oil longs have risen on expectations of continued supply disruption.

“Tuesday morning’s data shows a broad reduction in long bias across US equity indices as the price gains built on optimism that an eventual deal will be made took some buyers by surprise who opted to take profit, with a similar story occurring in gold,” said Monte Safieddine of Capital.com, adding that oil longs remain resilient while USD/JPY traders continue to favor the short side near the 160 level.

data.title: Charles River gains new Outperform at RBC despite NAMs related disruption
data.description:
Charles River Laboratories (CRL) gained on Tuesday as RBC Capital Markets launched its coverage with an Outperform recommendation, noting that the company is attractively valued despite disruption from New Alternative Methods ((NAMs)) for preclinical research.

Shares of the contract research organization dipped in March after the FDA issued draft guidance to help drug developers validate NAMs as the agency seeks to relax animal testing requirements in early stages of drug development.

“We think the NAMs evolution is taking shape and could be deflationary to traditional models,” RBC analyst Ryan Halsted wrote, noting it as a headwind to Charles River (CRL), which focuses on animal testing-based CRO activities.

“The use of NAMs is growing ~38% annually, according to our proprietary RBC Elements analysis, and is being facilitated by greater clarity into the regulatory pathway,” Halsted pointed out, noting that, however, Charles River (CRL) stock is pricing in too much of that risk.

According to the analyst, as the leading CRO, the company is well placed to benefit when the next cycle of preclinical R&D work is expected to accelerate into 2027.

“We expect preclinical R&D to improve in 2H26 and accelerate into 2027 as we approach the looming patent cliff," Halsted added, projecting CRL to record high-single-digit topline growth as a result, in line with prior 6%-8% long-term targets. The analyst issued a price target of $215 per share on the company.

data.title: CarMax signals $200M FY27 SG&A exit-rate cuts as it expects ~$400M capex and pauses buybacks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CarMax (KMX) Q4 FY2026

Management view

  • “During the fourth quarter, we made solid progress on the priorities outlined last call to strengthen the business,” including “lowering our prices, investing in acquisition marketing and deploying an initial set of digital enhancements designed to drive conversion,” said Thomas Folliard (Interim Executive Chairman).

  • “We improved our sales trends,” but “our EPS during the quarter was impacted by restructuring costs as well as by a noncash goodwill impairment,” said Enrique Mayor-Mora (Executive VP & CFO).

  • “We will put the customer at the heart of every decision we make to drive better performance,” and the initial priorities are to “make CarMax the obvious and easy choice,” “use technology to drive more differentiated experiences and efficiencies,” and “act with more urgency and intention,” said Keith Barr (CEO, President & Director).

  • “We expect our penetration growth targeting the top half of Tier 2 will accelerate in FY ’27,” and the redesigned MaxCare/MaxCare Plus rollout “has shown encouraging results across multiple markets to date,” said Jon Daniels (Senior Vice President).

Outlook

  • “As a guidepost for FY ’27, we currently expect used margins for the full year to decline at a rate broadly in line with our fourth quarter year-over-year trend,” and “we expect the first quarter to reflect the largest year-over-year decline at closer to $300 per unit,” said CFO Mayor-Mora.

  • “We have completed our EPP product redesign and testing and have begun our national rollout, which we expect will drive approximately $35 per unit in margins in FY ’27,” said Mayor-Mora.

  • “Regarding SG&A, we expect FY ’27 exit rate reductions of $200 million, an increase over the previous guidance of $150 million,” while “the year-over-year savings within FY ’27 are expected to be offset” by annualization of reduced corporate bonus/share-based comp, inflation, and new location growth, said Mayor-Mora.

  • “With leverage slightly above our targeted range… we have paused our share buybacks,” said Mayor-Mora.

Financial results

  • “During the quarter, we delivered total sales of $5.9 billion, down 1% compared to last year,” and “adjusted earnings per diluted share… was $0.34,” said Mayor-Mora.

  • “Fourth quarter net loss per diluted share was $0.85,” and “a noncash goodwill impairment of $0.99… and restructuring charges of $0.20… reduced EPS by $1.19 this quarter,” said Mayor-Mora.

  • “Total gross profit was $605 million,” and “profit per used unit… $2,115,” while “wholesale gross profit per unit… $940,” said Mayor-Mora.

  • “CarMax auto finance income of $144 million was down 10% year-over-year,” said Mayor-Mora.

  • “We repurchased 1.3 million shares for a total expenditure of $50 million,” and “we had $1.31 billion in repurchase authorization remaining,” said Mayor-Mora.

Q&A

  • Craig Kennison, Baird: asked about first observations and “streamlin[ing] the click-through experience”; CEO Barr said a focus is “reduce friction in the customer experience… If it takes us 6 clicks… how can we make it 3?”

  • Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer: pressed on price elasticity and what drove improved comps; CFO Mayor-Mora said “pricing certainly… had the biggest impact,” while also citing “increased… acquisition marketing” and “improvements to our online selling capabilities.”

  • Rajat Gupta, JPMorgan: asked how much of the $200 million SG&A hits FY27 and about store footprint pruning; CFO Mayor-Mora said FY26 exits “with about $100 million in savings” and FY27 adds “another $100 million,” with “full realization in FY ’28,” adding that footprint is “something that we’ll be assessing as part of the strategy.”

  • Sharon Zackfia, William Blair: asked about relaxing cosmetic standards and timing for a strategic plan; CFO Mayor-Mora said ValueMAX mix has risen and “we need to consider all potential levers,” but “we will not change… overall relative quality standards,” and added “in June, you’ll probably start to see some headlines.”

  • Scot Ciccarelli, Truist: asked about GPU floors and reconciling lower OpEx/CapEx with experience investment; CFO Mayor-Mora pointed to “reducing our COGS and logistics costs,” and CEO Barr said efficiency “doesn’t come at the expense of a great customer experience.”

  • Daniela Haigian, Morgan Stanley: asked for near-term digital changes and trackable metrics; CEO Barr said focus is to “reduce friction,” but metrics will align with strategy and management will “come back to you” after planning.

  • David Bellinger, Mizuho: asked about inventory and conversion; CFO Mayor-Mora said web traffic was “up like 14%,” “selling opportunities… relatively flat,” and “conversion was relatively flat,” while inventory levels and efficiency are part of strategy review.

  • Michael Montani, Evercore ISI: asked about delinquencies and Tier 2 mix; SVP Daniels said “delinquencies are higher, roll rates are higher,” but added “we’ve hit our losses right on the mark in Q3 and Q4” and Tier 2 penetration is expected to grow.

  • John Babcock, Barclays: asked capex reductions and Middle East tensions; CFO Mayor-Mora said capex cuts come from “new stores down from 6 to 4” and less real estate spend, while on macro risks the company will “control what we can control” with “a much more dynamic approach to margin management.”

  • Christopher Pierce, Needham: asked about sustaining competitiveness and logistics as a lever; CFO Mayor-Mora said being “more price competitive” is “nonnegotiable” and “logistics is an opportunity,” while SVP Daniels added CAF and EPP are profit levers.

  • John Healy, Northcoast: asked about CAF’s role and funding/structure; CEO Barr said “CAF is going to play a key piece,” and CFO Mayor-Mora said the company is exploring “alternative funding vehicles… whole loan sales,” while not expecting the revolver approach to change.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing, repeatedly probing pricing durability, SG&A timing, and credit stress, including questions on “a floor on GPU levels,” “pruning the number of locations,” and delinquency “roll rates.”

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and execution-focused, repeatedly emphasizing urgency and controllable levers, including “we are laser-focused” (CFO Mayor-Mora) and “we will put the customer at the heart of every decision” (CEO Barr).

  • Versus Q3, management language shifted from turnaround framing (“recent results have been unacceptable,” said Executive Chairman Folliard in Q3) to early traction plus near-term pressures (Q4: “we improved our sales trends,” said CFO Mayor-Mora) alongside a CEO transition and pending “June” strategic signals.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Leadership moved from an interim CEO search update in Q3 to a named CEO in Q4; Executive Chairman Folliard introduced Barr as “the right choice to lead CarMax.”

  • Q3 set an SG&A target of “at least $150 million” exit-rate savings; Q4 raised it to “$200 million” and added that “we have paused our share buybacks” due to leverage, per CFO Mayor-Mora.

  • Pricing actions described in Q3 as newly rolling out became a reported trend change in Q4, with CFO Mayor-Mora saying used unit comps were “down 1.9%” versus Q3’s “down 9%,” and Folliard reiterating, “price matters in this business.”

  • Analysts remained centered on pricing/elasticity and digital conversion in both quarters, but Q4 added more probing on inventory levels, macro shocks, and how quickly a new CEO will define metrics and targets.

Risks and concerns

  • “Delinquencies are higher, roll rates are higher,” and management cited “a stressed customer out there,” said SVP Daniels, with reserves positioned to reflect expected losses.

  • CFO Mayor-Mora flagged near-term earnings pressure from margin actions and noted external uncertainties including “inflationary pressures” and “the lowest consumer sentiment on record,” while emphasizing an ability to “react… pretty quickly” via dynamic margin management.

  • Management described ongoing pressure points in profitability drivers, including “service was pressured” and CAF income down year-over-year, alongside restructuring and impairment impacts on reported EPS.

Final takeaway

CarMax’s leadership framed Q4 as an inflection built on lower pricing, acquisition marketing, and early digital changes, while acknowledging near-term earnings pressure from a $0.99 goodwill impairment and restructuring costs. Management guided to further used-margin compression in FY27 (including a ~$300 per unit year-over-year decline in Q1), raised the FY27 SG&A exit-rate reduction target to $200 million, reduced planned FY27 capex to about $400 million, and paused buybacks as leverage sits above target. The new CEO emphasized simplifying the omnichannel journey and using technology, data, and AI to reduce friction, while CFO and CAF leadership highlighted margin opportunities from logistics/reconditioning efficiencies, expanded Tier 2 penetration, and the national rollout of redesigned MaxCare products.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nvidia adds Ising to its growing portfolio of open AI models
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) continues to expand its portfolio of open artificial intelligence models, with the latest, Ising, developed for quantum computing capabilities.

The Jensen Huang-led firm said it is the first family of open-source AI models for quantum computing. 

"AI is essential to making quantum computing practical," said Huang, founder and CEO of Nvidia. "With Ising, AI becomes the control plane—the operating system of quantum machines—transforming fragile qubits to scalable and reliable quantum-GPU systems."

The new models include Ising Calibration, a vision language model that can rapidly interpret and react to measurements from quantum processors. This enables AI agents to automate continuous calibration, reducing the time needed from days to hours.

The second is Ising Decoding, a 3D convolutional neural network model optimized for either speed or accuracy to perform real-time decoding for quantum error correction.

These new models are already being adopted by enterprises, academic institutions, and research labs for quantum computing development, Nvidia said. These range from IonQ (IONQ) to Cornell and Harvard to the Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory.

They also join Nvidia's open model portfolio, which includes Nemotron for agentic systems, Cosmos for physical AI, Alpamayo for autonomous vehicles, GROOT for robotics, and BioNeMo for biomedical research.

Nvidia expects the quantum computing market to surpass $11B by 2030.

data.title: Trump hints at imminent Iran talks in Pakistan
data.description:
President Donald Trump suggested Tuesday that negotiations with Iran could take place “over the next two days” in Pakistan, signaling a potential breakthrough in efforts to resolve the ongoing Middle East conflict.

In a call with a New York Post reporter, Trump credited Pakistan Field Marshal Gen. Asim Munir for facilitating the potential talks.

“You should stay there, really, because something could be happening over the next two days, and we’re more inclined to go there,” Trump said. “It’s more likely, you know why? Because the field marshal is doing a great job.”

Trump described Munir as “fantastic,” noting the strong relationship the two leaders developed during last year’s brief India-Pakistan conflict. The U.S. helped broker a peace deal that ended that four-day war.

“He’s fantastic, and therefore it’s more likely that we go back there,” Trump said. “Why should we go to some country that has nothing to do with it?”

Trump recently indicated that Iran had reached out for potential negotiations for a longer-term ceasefire, even as the U.S. moved forward with a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. Previous talks in Islamabad led by Vice President JD Vance failed to produce a breakthrough.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: TSLA, AMD, SNDK, AMZN
data.description:
The Asian Investor has upgraded Tesla (TSLA) to Buy, citing the recent selloff as an attractive entry point amid the company’s pivot to AI and robotics. Bashar Issa also sees opportunity in Amazon (AMZN), arguing the market is underestimating AWS’s strengthening position in generative AI through Bedrock and its Anthropic partnership.

On the downgrade side, James Foord has moved Sandisk (SNDK) to Hold after the stock surged nearly 20x from its spin-out price, warning of mounting risk factors including NAND cyclicality. The Alpha Analyst has also downgraded Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) from Buy to Hold, pointing to execution risks and a valuation that may have run ahead of fundamentals.

Upgrades

  • Tesla (TSLA): Upgrade to Buy by The Asian Investor. The analyst believes bearish sentiment and a 22% year-to-date decline have created an appealing entry point as Tesla accelerates its transformation toward AI, autonomous driving, and robotics.

    “I believe investors have become too bearish on Tesla’s growth prospects, and I view the current consolidation as an excellent opportunity to increase exposure to Tesla ahead of the first-quarter earnings report.”

  • Amazon.com (AMZN): Buy by Bashar Issa. The analyst argues that AWS is no longer on the defensive in AI, with Bedrock, OpenAI access, and a deepening Anthropic relationship strengthening its competitive position.

    “AMZN is reaping big rewards from its investment in Anthropic, which, through its Claude LLM, has risen through the ranks in the generative AI space. Its capabilities and features have captured attention, particularly from coders and app developers.”

Downgrades

  • Sandisk (SNDK): Downgrade to Hold by James Foord. Following a massive run-up approaching the analyst’s $1,000 price target, mounting risk factors including NAND cyclicality and speculative HBF technology warrant a more cautious stance.

    “NAND is undergoing an upgrade cycle, and this could severely limit pricing in the coming months and years, unless AI demand really expands...While process node improvements could be a headwind for NAND prices, the reality is that most of the capital is still being diverted toward HBM, and that keeps NAND structurally tighter than in previous cycles.”

  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD): Downgrade Buy to Hold by The Alpha Analyst. Despite robust Q4 2025 data center growth, the analyst cites rising execution risks, hyperscaler capex constraints, and weakening valuation support as reasons for caution.

    “AMD is up by almost 18% since the time of my last Buy call, and investors are advised to book some profits or at least await fresh capital deployment. This is an earlier than envisioned downgrade given the extent of upside I saw in November last year for long-term investors. The downgrade from a Buy to Hold acknowledges the change in market regime and its implications on valuations and returns.”

data.title: Brace for World Cup '26 inflation
data.description:
An analysis of the previous nine World Cups showed that hotel prices increased 72% year over year on average, according to Wells Fargo Securities.

This could translate into a 70-basis-point increase in the headline CPI figure, all else equal, according to Wells Fargo analysts.

 

 

 

The 2026 World Cup, which will be held in the U.S., Canada, and Mexico, is estimated to generate $10.9B in revenue, which includes broadcasting rights of over $3B, sponsorships, and ticket sales.

The last cup—the 2022 Qatar World Cup—brought over $7.5B in revenue. In hospitality and ticket sales, the cup brought $769M in revenue.

The 2026 cup is expected to be the most lucrative in history due to expansion to 48 teams, according to FIFA.
